The issue was this
Active actually means that the initialization of keys is done, and all subsequent pushes will be compressed.

When GC is not NONE, the sharding will be slightly different because originally we do original_size/num_servers, now we do compressed_size/num_servers (when sizes are not multiples of num_servers, rounding will cause issues). So even if GC is currently not active, we should use the second way of sharding, else a server won't have expected shard size.

The transaction **itself** is actually undamaged by misbehaving hooks:

- Exceptions raised from a before-commit hook don't prevent a subsequent attempt to commit the same transaction.
- Exceptions raised from an after-commit hook are logged and swallowed.

What is broken is the application's expectations about the transaction:  e.g. not all hooks get called, etc.  The point is that it is undefined behavior.

I started working on this, and there are a few API changes I'd like to make.  Before doing so, I wanted to run them by you:
- Change the function name from `makeObj` to `makeGalaxy` since these are always galaxies.
- Have `makeGalaxy` take a `gal_type` parameter, rather than fix it at the time of `COSMOSCatalog` creation.
- The constructor would still take a `use_real` parameter to let people opt out of using any real galaxies, in which case the `gal_type` parameter for `makeGalaxy` defaults to 'parametric', since that's the only option.
- Change the name of the `pad_size` parameter to `noise_pad_size` to match what it is called in RealGalaxy.  Seems better to use the same name for the same concept.
- Don't multiply this number by sqrt(2).  I think I see the logic of why you did that, but I think it's better to just explain to people how they should calculate a good value for `noise_pad_size` rather than take what they give and then make it larger.
- Change the default `noise_pad_size` to 0.4 to accommodate the fact that 0.25 won't by multiplied by sqrt(2) anymore.  (Or should this be 0.35?  Or 0.5 if these sound to precise for the level of arbitrariness involved?)
- Change the name of `indices` to `index`.  Still allow it to be a list though.
- Have `makeObj` use the provided `rng` for noise padding as well as random index selection.
- Use the name 'COSMOSGalaxy' for this type of object in config.  There would be no corresponding python class.  It would just be the output of `COSMOSCatalog.makeGalaxy()`.

Feel free to push back on any of these if you don't like them.  

I'm planning to add this feature to demo11 for the galaxy selection, since the current galaxy choice is basically a rehash of demo10, except for the noise_pad_size option, so it would be fine to switch to COSMOSGalaxy there.  I was thinking of using 'RandomBinomial' to randomly select some fraction to be real and the rest parametric, since we don't demo that option anywhere yet.  Also it will be faster if we have some parametric galaxies than if they are all real.

Why not use `*args`?  This would save you from having to construct a list to call the function, which would make it cleaner, IMO:
```Python
def no_upcast(*args):
    result_list = []
     for element in args:
         if isinstance(element, np.ndarray):
             result_list.append(T.constant(element))
         else:
             result_list.append(element)
     return result_list
```
and the call signature would be e.g.
```
learning_rate, rho, epsilon = utils.no_upcast(
    learning_rate, rho, epsilon)
```

... or, better yet, make it a pretty one-liner with a list comprehension
```
def no_upcast(*args):
     return (T.constant(e) if isinstance(e, np.ndarray) else e for e in args)
```

```python
def foo(test=None):

var = False
foo(var)
print(var)
```
will *always* print `False`.

I mention this because an earlier theme I saw was moving getter/setter operations into the C++ headers.

Thus if this member function has the potential to be used more then once, then this should be a a CPP file not the header file. My point is this, using a get function as an example, is about 2 opcodes. Often it is a single opcode because a register has been previously allocated to the "this" pointer

```
    ldr  r0,_this_pointer
    str  r1,[r0,#member_offset]
```
That is almost the same cost as a subroutine call. The advantage is the compiler can optimize register allocation across that inlined getter/setter function.

This member function has potentially 3 subroutine calls.

These little things add up...  Death by many paper cuts.

It's the same approach that we talked about. We add a new scanning mode `SPINEL_SCAN_STATE_DISCOVER` which starts the discover scan operation. The discover operation uses/shares the parameter `SPINEL_PROP_MAC_SCAN_MASK` to decide the channel mask and also uses the newly added `PROP_THREAD_DISOVERY_REQUEST_JOINER_FLAG` and `PROP_THREAD_DISCOVERY_SCAN_ENABLE_FILTERING` as input argument to configure the discover scan operation.

Added the property `PROP_THREAD_DISCOVER_SCAN_RESULT` to emit the discovery scan result. The data items in this property are almost the same as `SPINEL_PROP_MAC_SCAN_BEACON` but with discover result we also have steering data as part of net layer properties which does not really apply in case of active scan.

The key question is if we want to reuse the `SCAN_BEACON` for both active and discover scan result or define a new one like `PROP_THREAD_DISCOVER_SCAN_RESULT`. Both ways seem ok to me. Slight upside of defining a new property is that it can help us distinguish the result and we can in future add new sub-property items to either one independently.

A possibly working but extremely dirty hack may be using:
```
    version_cmd=['/bin/sh', '-c', '. /etc/conf.d/apache2; apache2 ${APACHE2_OPTS} -v'],
    define_cmd=['/bin/sh', '-c', '. /etc/conf.d/apache2; apache2 ${APACHE2_OPTS} -S'],
    restart_cmd=['/bin/sh', '-c', '. /etc/conf.d/apache2; apache2 ${APACHE2_OPTS} -k graceful'],
    conftest_cmd=['/bin/sh', '-c', '. /etc/conf.d/apache2; apache2 ${APACHE2_OPTS} -t'],
```
In short, this "config" plays inception with `subprocess.Popen` (which, in the end, executes what is defined in `CLI_DEFAULTS_GENTOO`) by making it start a shell that itself starts a shell that sources `/etc/conf.d/apache2` and then executes the apache2 command with the sourced `APACHE2_OPTS` (just like `/etc/init.d/apache2` and `/usr/lib/systemd/system/apache2.service` do).

It passed my tests in a VM but since I have no complete test setup for this project running I cannot confirm that it will work in every situation so if you (@osirisinferi) can take over the formal testing, then we may have a solution that will work for both of us AND enables everyone to use the automatic installation.

But still, it would be a dirty hack that should be removed as soon as certbot supports `CLI_DEFAULTS` depending on the used init system.

```
        self.assertRaises(PluginError, self.auth.perform, [self.achall])
```
This works because `self.config` refers to the same object in the authenticator. You can also do `self.auth.config.google_credentials = None` which may make this cleaner/clearer.

I just think we can reuse the `self.auth` object already created for us by the `setUp` function.
This appears to be called `python2-certbot-apache`. I created certbot/website#155 to fix the issue on the website.

I was specifically referring to a class variable like `OS_DEFAULTS` instead an instance variable to work around this.

It's all a tradeoff though. Variables with a large scope can cause problems but so can trying to remember to properly make changes to multiple places in the future. A comment is fine for now.
Since this is in markdown, the names should just link to the respective staff profiles. If someone is being harassed and they need to report it to us, we should make it as easy as we possibly can.

I felt that it is a bit orthogonal to the main functionality of `download()`. Though, I agree, I have found this functionality useful quite often myself, too. Since the `.zip` files can now be expected to be complete and verified with this PR's changes, there is normally no need to recheck existing files.
There are a couple of options:
* Add a separate method for this (`checksum()`?) functionality, which takes a list of `id`s as input.
* Not implement this at all. The zip files can be verified quite easily with `zip -T` or `7z t` commands or via a GUI.
* Re-add it to `download()`. Since you are likely interested in fixing the broken files rather than just deleting them, `download()` is actually a quite sensible place for this functionality.

I guess `download()` is still the best place for this feature. :)
